findings.title: Hindi half of emergency phrases unspeakable when backend connected â€” pyttsx3 path has no Hindi voice and language is dropped
findings.scenario: Patient is on the web-browsing screen and dwells the emergency button. WebBrowsingScreen.handleEmergency (line 3629) UNCONDITIONALLY speaks `${english} ${hindi}` â€” Devanagari 'à¤®à¥à¤à¥‡ à¤¤à¥à¤°à¤‚à¤¤ à¤®à¤¦à¤¦ à¤šà¤¾à¤¹à¤¿à¤à¥¤ à¤¯à¤¹ à¤†à¤ªà¤¾à¤¤à¤•à¤¾à¤²à¥€à¤¨ à¤¸à¥à¤¥à¤¿à¤¤à¤¿ à¤¹à¥ˆà¥¤' is always appended, regardless of showHindi. With the backend connected (the normal full-app state), handleSpeak now routes ONLY to ws.speak. The backend SAPI5 English voice cannot render Devanagari, so the Hindi half of the distress call comes out silent or as garbage. A Hindi-speaking caregiver in another room no longer hears the Hindi plea. Same loss on GlobalNavBar.handleEmergency with showHindi=true (line 198-201) and for any Devanagari text the patient types and speaks with ttsLanguage='hindi'/'auto'. Before this commit the browser voice also fired and, per the documented auto-detect (App.tsx:149-156: `u.lang = /[à¤€-à¥¿]/.test(text) ? 'hi-IN' : 'en-US'`), spoke the Hindi intelligibly via a Windows hi-IN voice; now that voice is suppressed whenever the backend is up.
findings.evidence: ws.speak sends text only: useWebSocket.tsx:608 `speak: (text) => send('speak', { text })` â€” the ttsLanguage setting never crosses the wire. Backend handler main.py:1860 `'speak': lambda: self._speak(data.get('text', ''))` â†’ TTSEngine.speak â†’ `self.engine.say(value)` with no language/voice switching anywhere. The only voice logic in the backend is main.py:697-699 `if 'female' in voice.name.lower(): setProperty('voice', voice.id)` â€” SAPI voice names are e.g. 'Microsoft Zira Desktop - English (United States)' / 'Microsoft Hemant - Hindi (India)', so this matches nothing and the default en-US voice is used; even with the Windows Hindi voice pack installed it is never selected. Meanwhile the suppressed frontend path (App.tsx:150-156) explicitly set hi-IN for 'hindi' and auto-detected Devanagari for 'auto'. The Settings panel still offers 'Hindi' and 'Auto-detect' (AppSettingsPanel.tsx:635-639), which are now no-ops in connected operation. CLAUDE.md lists 'Bilingual: English + Hindi' as a core requirement and 'any regression in the emergency path is CRITICAL'.

findings.title: Backend 'connected' but voiceless yields total silence â€” no fallback trigger, no error feedback, emergency included
findings.scenario: pyttsx3 fails to initialize (SAPI/COM init errors are a known pyttsx3 failure mode; also TTS_AVAILABLE=False if the package is missing, or ServerConfig.tts_enabled=False as used by the test rigs). The WebSocket is perfectly healthy, so ws.isConnected===true and handleSpeak takes the ws.speak branch on every press. The backend merely logs 'TTS not available. Would speak: ...' and produces no sound and no error message back to the UI. The patient presses the persistent emergency button (App.tsx:112) â€” silence. Every other speak â€” silence. Nothing on screen indicates failure. Before this commit the browser voice always fired in parallel, so this exact backend failure was inaudible to the patient; the diff converts it into a total mute. Additionally, a press landing in the window where the socket has died but onclose/state hasn't propagated is silently swallowed: send() no-ops when readyState !== OPEN, and the fallback branch is not taken because isConnected is still stale-true.
findings.evidence: main.py:713-719 worker loop: `if self.engine: ... else: logger.warning(f"TTS not available. Would speak: {value}")` â€” log only. main.py:703-705: init failure leaves `self.engine = None`. main.py:2560-2566 `_speak` calls tts.speak + logging and sends NOTHING back to the websocket client (contrast `_get_fatigue` at 2568 which does `self._send(...)`), so the frontend cannot detect the failure and fall back. useWebSocket.tsx:264-268: `if (wsRef.current?.readyState === WebSocket.OPEN) { ...send... }` â€” silent drop otherwise; isConnected is React state set only in onclose (line 501-503), so it lags actual socket death. handleSpeak (App.tsx:245-249) has no retry, no timeout, no acknowledgement â€” `ws.speak(text)` is fire-and-forget into a path that can be a no-op while reporting connected.

findings.title: Voice settings (Volume '0 = muted', Rate, Language) become dead controls in normal connected operation â€” backend sync was never wired
findings.scenario: The commit's premise is that the backend is now the sole voice when connected â€” but the backend never receives the patient's TTS settings. ws.setTTSRate and ws.setTTSVolume are defined (useWebSocket.tsx:610-611) yet have ZERO call sites anywhere in src/ (verified by grep), and ws.speak carries text only. The backend therefore always speaks at its hardcoded init values rate=150, volume=1.0 (main.py:690-691). The Settings panel exposes Volume with the explicit description 'Voice volume level (0 = muted)' (AppSettingsPanel.tsx:656), Speech Rate 80-250 WPM, and Language â€” at night the patient drags Volume to 0 expecting mute, yet every speak still plays at full volume; the default customization even ships ttsVolume: 0.25 (defaultCustomization.ts:592) while the patient actually hears 100%. Before the commit, these settings at least governed the audible browser utterance (speakText honors volume on a matching 0-1 scale and returns early at volume<=0, App.tsx:144); the diff suppresses the only voice that consumed them, so the entire Voice section now does nothing until the backend goes down.
findings.evidence: Grep over src/ for setTTSRate|setTTSVolume returns only the interface declaration (useWebSocket.tsx:117-118) and the implementation (610-611) â€” no caller. The commit message says the backend 'is now the sole voice when connected' but adds no settings synchronization, making the never-wired sync load-bearing for the first time. speakText's settings handling (App.tsx:143-159: `if (volume <= 0) return; u.rate = rate; u.volume = volume; u.lang = ...`) is now reachable only when ws.isConnected is false. (Side note weakening the fallback too: the Rate slider stores WPM 80-250 which speakText passes raw into SpeechSynthesisUtterance.rate whose valid range is 0.1-10 â€” so the offline fallback voice is also clamped to maximum speed once the slider is ever touched; pre-existing, but it means neither path honors rate.)

findings.title: Double-speak still possible across connection transitions â€” ws branch never cancels an in-flight browser utterance
findings.scenario: Backend restarts (reconnect backoff begins at 1s, useWebSocket.tsx:504). While disconnected, the patient speaks a long sentence â€” the browser fallback utterance starts. The backend reconnects mid-utterance. The patient dwells the next phrase (or the emergency button): handleSpeak now takes the ws.speak branch, the backend voice starts immediately, and the still-playing browser utterance overlaps it â€” two simultaneous voices, the exact symptom commit b097efb claims to have eliminated. The same happens at app startup if the Python backend comes up a few seconds after the renderer and the patient speaks during the gap.
findings.evidence: window.speechSynthesis.cancel() exists only inside speakText (App.tsx:146), i.e. only on the fallback branch. The primary branch (App.tsx:245-246) is `if (ws.isConnected && ws.speak) { ws.speak(text); }` with no `window.speechSynthesis.cancel()` before it, so a fallback utterance started seconds earlier keeps playing under the backend voice. A one-line cancel in the ws branch closes the gap.
